text: ================================================================================ GDPR ACCESS CONTROL REQUIREMENTS ================================================================================ OVERVIEW -------- The GDPR does not prescribe a specific named "access control" standard but establishes access control obligations across several articles. The core requirement is that personal data must only be accessible to those who need it, that access must be technically enforced, and that the controller must be able to demonstrate compliance. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 5 - PRINCIPLES RELATING TO PROCESSING OF PERSONAL DATA -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 5(1)(f) - Integrity and Confidentiality (Security Principle): Personal data shall be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures ('integrity and confidentiality'). Article 5(2) - Accountability: The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance with, paragraph 1 ('accountability'). Cloud Implication: - Storage services (S3, Blob, GCS) must have public access disabled. - IAM roles must enforce least-privilege access to personal data. - Controllers must be able to produce evidence of these controls on demand. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 25 - DATA PROTECTION BY DESIGN AND BY DEFAULT -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 25(1): Taking into account the state of the art, the cost of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risks of varying likelihood and severity for rights and freedoms of natural persons posed by the processing, the controller shall, both at the time of the determination of the means for processing and at the time of the processing itself, implement appropriate technical and organisational measures, such as pseudonymisation, which are designed to implement data-protection principles, such as data minimisation, in an effective manner and to integrate the necessary safeguards into the processing in order to meet the requirements of this Regulation and protect the rights of data subjects. Article 25(2) - Data Protection by Default: The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures for ensuring that, by default, only personal data which are necessary for each specific purpose of the processing are processed. That obligation applies to the amount of personal data collected, the extent of their processing, the period of their storage and their accessibility. In particular, such measures shall ensure that by default personal data are not made accessible without the individual's intervention to an indefinite number of natural persons. Cloud Implication: - By default, cloud storage must NOT be publicly accessible. -

that, by default, only personal data which are necessary for each specific purpose of the processing are processed. That obligation applies to the amount of personal data collected, the extent of their processing, the period of their storage and their accessibility. In particular, such measures shall ensure that by default personal data are not made accessible without the individual's intervention to an indefinite number of natural persons. Cloud Implication: - By default, cloud storage must NOT be publicly accessible. - New resources should default to the most restrictive access configuration. - IAM policies must not use wildcard (*) principals unless explicitly justified. - Network-level access must be restricted by default (private endpoints, VPC-only). -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 32 - SECURITY OF PROCESSING -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 32(1): Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including as appropriate: (a) the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; (c) the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; (d) a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing. Article 32(2): In assessing the appropriate level of security, account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to, personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. Article 32(4): The controller and processor shall take steps to ensure that any natural person acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data does not process them except on instructions from the controller, unless he or she is required to do so by Union or Member State law. Cloud Implication: - All access to personal data must be role-based and follow least privilege. - Service accounts must not have overly broad permissions. - Human access to production data must be audited and minimised. - Cross-account or cross-service

acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data does not process them except on instructions from the controller, unless he or she is required to do so by Union or Member State law. Cloud Implication: - All access to personal data must be role-based and follow least privilege. - Service accounts must not have overly broad permissions. - Human access to production data must be audited and minimised. - Cross-account or cross-service access must be explicitly authorized. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 28 - PROCESSOR OBLIGATIONS (ACCESS BY THIRD PARTIES) -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 28(1): Where processing is to be carried out on behalf of a controller, the controller shall use only processors providing sufficient guarantees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that processing will meet the requirements of this Regulation and ensure the protection of the rights of the data subject. Article 28(3)(b): The processor shall process personal data only on documented instructions from the controller, including with regard to transfers of personal data to a third country. Article 28(3)(f): The processor shall assist the controller in ensuring compliance with the obligations pursuant to Articles 32 to 36 (security, breach notification, DPIA, prior consultation). Cloud Implication: - Cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) must be engaged under a Data Processing Agreement. - Access by cloud provider support staff to customer personal data must be restricted and must require documented customer authorization. - Sub-processors used by cloud providers must provide equivalent access controls. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 29 - PROCESSING UNDER THE AUTHORITY OF THE CONTROLLER OR PROCESSOR -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 29: The processor and any person acting under the authority of the controller or of the processor who has access to personal data shall not process those data except on instructions from the controller, unless required to do so by Union or Member State law. Cloud Implication: - Internal users (developers, analysts) with IAM access to personal data must operate under documented, controller-defined access policies. - Break-glass / emergency access to personal data must be logged and justified. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- RECITAL 39 - TRANSPARENCY AND CONFIDENTIALITY -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Recital 39: Personal data should be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security and confidentiality of the personal data, including for preventing unauthorised access to or use of personal data and the equipment used for the processing. Cloud Implication: - Network controls (security groups, NACLs, firewall rules) must

to personal data must operate under documented, controller-defined access policies. - Break-glass / emergency access to personal data must be logged and justified. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- RECITAL 39 - TRANSPARENCY AND CONFIDENTIALITY -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Recital 39: Personal data should be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security and confidentiality of the personal data, including for preventing unauthorised access to or use of personal data and the equipment used for the processing. Cloud Implication: - Network controls (security groups, NACLs, firewall rules) must prevent unauthorized network-level access to systems that process personal data. - Compute instances processing personal data must not have unrestricted inbound rules. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- RECITAL 78 - DATA PROTECTION BY DESIGN -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Recital 78: In order to be able to demonstrate compliance with this Regulation, the controller should adopt internal policies and implement measures which meet in particular the principles of data protection by design and data protection by default. Such measures could consist, inter alia, of minimising the processing of personal data, pseudonymising personal data as soon as possible, transparency with regard to the functions and processing of personal data, enabling the data subject to monitor the data processing, enabling the controller to create and improve security features. Cloud Implication: - New services should be deployed with access restrictions as the default configuration. - Developers should not be able to bypass access controls in production environments. - Infrastructure-as-code (Terraform) must encode access policies, not leave them open. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PRACTICAL CLOUD CONTROLS DERIVED FROM GDPR ACCESS REQUIREMENTS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 1. Storage (S3 / Blob Storage / GCS): - Block all public access at the account and bucket level. - Use bucket policies or access control lists only to grant minimum required access. - Enable server-side encryption with access to keys restricted to authorized roles. - Disable ACLs where uniform access management is supported. 2. Compute (EC2 / Virtual Machines / Compute Engine): - Use IAM instance profiles instead of embedding access keys. - Disable instance metadata service v1 (IMDSv1) on AWS; require IMDSv2. - Do not assign public IP addresses to instances handling personal data unless required. - Use VPC/VNet to isolate personal data processing workloads. 3. Database (RDS / PostgreSQL / Cloud SQL): - Disable public accessibility. - Use IAM authentication where supported. - Restrict network access to application-tier CIDR ranges only. - Rotate database credentials regularly. 4. - Disable instance metadata service v1 (IMDSv1) on AWS; require IMDSv2. - Do not assign public IP addresses to instances handling personal data unless required. - Use VPC/VNet to isolate personal data processing workloads. 3. Database (RDS / PostgreSQL / Cloud SQL): - Disable public accessibility. - Use IAM authentication where supported. - Restrict network access to application-tier CIDR ranges only. - Rotate database credentials regularly. 4. Identity (IAM / Azure AD / Cloud IAM): - Enforce MFA for all accounts accessing personal data. - Apply least-privilege role assignments. - Review and revoke unused permissions on a defined schedule. - Use service accounts with scoped permissions; avoid user-level credentials in services. 5. Serverless / Functions: - Restrict inbound triggers to authenticated sources. - Assign execution roles with only the permissions required for the function's purpose. - Do not expose functions publicly if they process personal data without authentication. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PENALTIES FOR NON-COMPLIANCE WITH ACCESS CONTROLS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 83(4): Infringements of obligations related to Article 25 (data protection by design and by default) and Article 32 (security of processing) shall be subject to administrative fines of up to EUR 10,000,000, or in the case of an undertaking, up to 2% of the total worldwide annual turnover of the preceding financial year, whichever is higher. Article 83(5): Infringements of the basic principles for processing, including conditions for consent, (Articles 5, 6, 7, and 9) shall be subject to administrative fines of up to EUR 20,000,000, or in the case of an undertaking, up to 4% of the total worldwide annual turnover of the preceding financial year, whichever is higher.

================================================================================ GDPR — AUDIT EVIDENCE AND ACCOUNTABILITY ================================================================================ OVERVIEW -------- The GDPR places a strong, active obligation on data controllers and processors to not only comply with the regulation but to be able to demonstrate that compliance at any time — including during regulatory audits, investigations, and civil disputes. This is known as the accountability principle. It is not sufficient to implement controls silently; organizations must maintain documentary evidence that those controls exist and function as intended. The audit evidence requirements under GDPR are spread across several articles. The most relevant are: - Article 5(2): Accountability principle - Article 24: Responsibility of the controller - Article 25: Data protection by design and by default - Article 28: Processor obligations and contracts - Article 30: Records of processing activities (RoPA) - Article 32: Security of processing and regular testing - Article 33(5): Documentation of all personal data breaches - Article 35: Data protection impact assessments (DPIAs) - Article 37–39: Data Protection Officer (DPO) tasks and position - Article 58: Supervisory authority investigative powers Each of these articles generates a specific audit evidence obligation described in detail below. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 5(2) — THE ACCOUNTABILITY PRINCIPLE -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text: "The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance with, paragraph 1." This is the foundational audit obligation in the GDPR. It means: 1. Compliance is the controller's active responsibility — not a passive state. 2. The controller must be able to demonstrate compliance upon request. 3. Demonstration normally requires written documentation, records, policies, and logs rather than oral assertions alone. The accountability principle does not specify exactly how compliance must be demonstrated. This is intentional: the required documentation varies depending on the nature, scale, and risk level of the processing operation. In most cases, compliance is demonstrated through a combination of: - Records of processing activities (Article 30) - Internal data protection policies - Data protection impact assessments (Article 35) - Processor contracts (Article 28) - Staff training records - Consent records (Article 7) - Security audit results (Article 32) - Breach logs (Article 33(5)) Cloud security implication: Any cloud service processing personal data on behalf of an organization must support the controller's ability to demonstrate compliance. Logging must be enabled. Access records must be maintained. Configuration changes that affect data protection must be auditable. Cloud providers acting as processors must cooperate with audits under Article 28. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 24

- Processor contracts (Article 28) - Staff training records - Consent records (Article 7) - Security audit results (Article 32) - Breach logs (Article 33(5)) Cloud security implication: Any cloud service processing personal data on behalf of an organization must support the controller's ability to demonstrate compliance. Logging must be enabled. Access records must be maintained. Configuration changes that affect data protection must be auditable. Cloud providers acting as processors must cooperate with audits under Article 28. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 24 — RESPONSIBILITY OF THE CONTROLLER -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (summary): The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure and be able to demonstrate that processing is performed in accordance with the GDPR. Key obligations for audit evidence: 1. Implement and maintain documented data protection policies. 2. Review and update those policies when necessary and proportionate. 3. Adhere to approved codes of conduct (Article 40) or certification mechanisms (Article 42) as a means of demonstrating compliance. Article 24 makes clear that having documented measures is part of compliance itself — not just evidence of it. A controller that cannot produce documentation is non-compliant regardless of whether its processing is substantively correct. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 25 — DATA PROTECTION BY DESIGN AND BY DEFAULT -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (summary): The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures designed to implement data-protection principles in an effective manner and to integrate the necessary safeguards into the processing. Audit evidence this generates: - Design-time documentation showing that data minimisation was considered - Justification for each data field collected - Evidence that default settings are the most privacy-protective available - Records of architecture and configuration decisions at the system design phase Cloud security implication: When recommending cloud configuration fields, enabling privacy-protective defaults (e.g., block public access, disable unauthenticated access, enforce encryption) is a direct expression of Article 25. Documentation of why each default was chosen constitutes audit evidence. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 28 — PROCESSOR OBLIGATIONS AND CONTRACTS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (summary): Where processing is to be carried out on behalf of a controller, the controller shall use only processors providing sufficient guarantees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures. Audit evidence this generates: - Written contracts or Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with all processors - Evidence that the processor's compliance was assessed before engagement - Documentation of the scope of processing permitted - Sub-processor agreements and approval records - Audit rights clause in the DPA, and

CONTRACTS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (summary): Where processing is to be carried out on behalf of a controller, the controller shall use only processors providing sufficient guarantees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures. Audit evidence this generates: - Written contracts or Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with all processors - Evidence that the processor's compliance was assessed before engagement - Documentation of the scope of processing permitted - Sub-processor agreements and approval records - Audit rights clause in the DPA, and records of exercising those rights Controllers are liable for processor non-compliance if they failed to perform adequate due diligence. Evidence of due diligence (vendor assessments, contract reviews, periodic audits) is therefore critical. Cloud security implication: AWS, Azure, and GCP all offer Data Processing Agreements. Enabling audit logging on cloud services so that processor-level activity can be reviewed is a direct requirement flowing from Article 28 audit rights obligations. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 30 — RECORDS OF PROCESSING ACTIVITIES (RoPA) -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (paragraph 1): "Each controller and, where applicable, the controller's representative, shall maintain a record of processing activities under its responsibility. That record shall contain all of the following information: (a) the name and contact details of the controller and, where applicable, the joint controller, the controller's representative and the DPO; (b) the purposes of the processing; (c) a description of the categories of data subjects and personal data; (d) the categories of recipients to whom personal data have been or will be disclosed, including recipients in third countries or international organisations; (e) where applicable, transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation; (f) where possible, the envisaged time limits for erasure of different categories of data; (g) where possible, a general description of the technical and organisational security measures referred to in Article 32(1)." Full text (paragraph 2): Processors must maintain an equivalent record of processing categories carried out on behalf of each controller, including: (a) the name and contact details of the processor and each controller; (b) the categories of processing carried out on behalf of each controller; (c) where applicable, transfers to third countries; (d) where possible, a general description of the security measures. Full text (paragraph 3): These records shall be in writing, including electronic form. Full text (paragraph 4): The controller or processor and, where applicable, the controller's or processor's representative, shall make the record available to the supervisory authority on request.

Full text (paragraph 3): These records shall be in writing, including electronic form. Full text (paragraph 4): The controller or processor and, where applicable, the controller's or processor's representative, shall make the record available to the supervisory authority on request. Exceptions (paragraph 5): The obligation does not apply to organizations with fewer than 250 employees unless the processing is likely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of data subjects, unless the processing is not occasional, or unless the processing includes special categories (Article 9) or criminal conviction data (Article 10). Cloud security implication: Cloud-based processing activities (e.g., storing customer data in S3, processing transactions via RDS, running user authentication via IAM) must all be documented in the RoPA. The record should describe the cloud service, the data involved, the retention period, and the security measures in place. This is a mandatory document that must be producible on supervisory authority request. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 32(1)(d) — REGULAR TESTING AND EVALUATION OF SECURITY MEASURES -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text: "...a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing." This sub-clause of Article 32 is specifically audit-relevant because it requires organizations to not only implement security measures but to periodically verify that those measures are working as intended. Audit evidence this generates: - Penetration testing reports and remediation records - Vulnerability scanning results and patch logs - Security review records for cloud configurations - Results of regular access reviews and privilege audits - Records of security control effectiveness assessments Cloud security implication: Enabling services such as AWS Config, AWS Security Hub, Azure Security Center, or GCP Security Command Center satisfies the intent of Article 32(1)(d) by providing ongoing, documented evaluation of cloud security configurations. Logs and alert histories from these services are audit evidence. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 33(5) — DOCUMENTATION OF PERSONAL DATA BREACHES -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text: "The controller shall document any personal data breaches, comprising the facts relating to the personal data breach, its effects and the remedial action taken. That documentation shall enable the supervisory authority to verify compliance with this Article." This is one of the most

satisfies the intent of Article 32(1)(d) by providing ongoing, documented evaluation of cloud security configurations. Logs and alert histories from these services are audit evidence. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 33(5) — DOCUMENTATION OF PERSONAL DATA BREACHES -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text: "The controller shall document any personal data breaches, comprising the facts relating to the personal data breach, its effects and the remedial action taken. That documentation shall enable the supervisory authority to verify compliance with this Article." This is one of the most specific audit evidence requirements in the GDPR. All personal data breaches must be documented regardless of whether they are notified to the supervisory authority. The breach register must contain: - Date and time of breach discovery - Description of the breach (what happened) - Categories and approximate number of data subjects affected - Categories and approximate number of personal data records affected - Description of likely consequences of the breach - Description of remedial measures taken or proposed - Reason for not notifying the supervisory authority, if notification was not made (i.e., why the breach was unlikely to result in risk) Cloud security implication: All cloud-side security incidents affecting personal data must be logged and documented. Enabling CloudTrail (AWS), Azure Monitor, or GCP Cloud Audit Logs ensures there is a technical record of unauthorized access, data exfiltration attempts, or misconfiguration events that may constitute a breach. These logs feed into the mandatory breach register. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 35 — DATA PROTECTION IMPACT ASSESSMENT (DPIA) -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (summary): Where a type of processing is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller shall carry out a data protection impact assessment prior to the processing. A DPIA must include: 1. A systematic description of the envisaged processing operations and their purposes 2. An assessment of the necessity and proportionality of the processing 3. An assessment of the risks to the rights and freedoms of data subjects 4. The measures envisaged to address the risks DPIAs are mandatory for: - Systematic profiling with legal or similarly significant effects - Large-scale processing of special categories of data (Article 9) - Large-scale, systematic monitoring of a publicly accessible area DPIAs are strong audit evidence that demonstrates the organization thought through data protection risks before processing began, rather than retroactively. Cloud security implication: New cloud deployments processing personal data at scale — particularly those involving customer databases,

of data subjects 4. The measures envisaged to address the risks DPIAs are mandatory for: - Systematic profiling with legal or similarly significant effects - Large-scale processing of special categories of data (Article 9) - Large-scale, systematic monitoring of a publicly accessible area DPIAs are strong audit evidence that demonstrates the organization thought through data protection risks before processing began, rather than retroactively. Cloud security implication: New cloud deployments processing personal data at scale — particularly those involving customer databases, health records, financial data, or behavioral analytics — should have a DPIA. The DPIA should document the cloud services used, the security controls applied, and the residual risks accepted. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 37–39 — DATA PROTECTION OFFICER (DPO) -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Organizations required to appoint a DPO must document: - The identity and contact details of the DPO - Evidence that the DPO was involved in all data protection matters - Records of DPO advice and the organization's response to that advice Article 39 tasks the DPO with monitoring compliance. DPO activity logs and reports are audit evidence that an active compliance monitoring function exists. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 58 — SUPERVISORY AUTHORITY INVESTIGATIVE POWERS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Supervisory authorities have broad powers to request audit evidence directly, including: - Ordering the controller to provide any information needed for the authority's tasks - Carrying out investigations in the form of data protection audits - Obtaining access to all personal data and information necessary to perform its tasks - Obtaining access to the controller's or processor's premises and processing equipment These powers mean that the documentary record described across Articles 5(2), 24, 28, 30, 32, 33, and 35 must be organized and accessible, not just stored somewhere. The supervisory authority can and does request these documents during investigations and routine audits. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- CLOUD CONFIGURATION IMPLICATIONS — SUMMARY -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- GDPR audit evidence requirements translate directly into cloud configuration recommendations: 1. ENABLE AUDIT LOGGING ON ALL SERVICES - AWS: CloudTrail (API audit), S3 access logs, RDS audit logs, Lambda logs in CloudWatch, CloudFront access logs - Azure: Azure Monitor, Activity Log, Diagnostic Logs for each service - GCP: Cloud Audit Logs (Admin Activity, Data Access, System Event) - Rationale: Article 30 (RoPA), Article 32(1)(d) (effectiveness testing), Article 33(5) (breach documentation) 2. RETAIN LOGS FOR A DEFINED PERIOD - Log retention period should be documented and justified based on purpose - Minimum recommendation for GDPR audit trail: 12 months (operational), longer

(API audit), S3 access logs, RDS audit logs, Lambda logs in CloudWatch, CloudFront access logs - Azure: Azure Monitor, Activity Log, Diagnostic Logs for each service - GCP: Cloud Audit Logs (Admin Activity, Data Access, System Event) - Rationale: Article 30 (RoPA), Article 32(1)(d) (effectiveness testing), Article 33(5) (breach documentation) 2. RETAIN LOGS FOR A DEFINED PERIOD - Log retention period should be documented and justified based on purpose - Minimum recommendation for GDPR audit trail: 12 months (operational), longer for breach investigation evidence - Rationale: Article 30(1)(f) (envisaged time limits), Article 33(5) 3. PROTECT LOG INTEGRITY - Logs must not be alterable by application-level users - Use write-once storage (S3 Object Lock, Azure immutable storage, GCP WORM) - Rationale: Article 5(1)(f) (integrity and confidentiality) 4. RESTRICT ACCESS TO LOGS - Logs containing personal data are themselves personal data under GDPR - Access should be limited to authorized personnel - MFA should be enforced for access to audit trail systems - Rationale: Article 25 (data protection by design), Article 32 5. DOCUMENT CLOUD SECURITY CONFIGURATIONS - Configuration decisions (e.g., "encryption enabled because Article 32") should be recorded - Rationale: Article 24, Article 5(2) accountability 6. ENABLE VULNERABILITY AND COMPLIANCE MONITORING - AWS Config, Azure Policy, GCP Security Command Center provide ongoing compliance monitoring records - These outputs are audit evidence under Article 32(1)(d) - Rationale: Article 32(1)(d) regular testing and evaluation 7. MAINTAIN A BREACH REGISTER - All cloud security incidents affecting personal data must be logged - Even non-notifiable incidents must be documented - Rationale: Article 33(5) 8. DOCUMENT PROCESSOR RELATIONSHIPS - Each cloud provider acting as a data processor must have a DPA in place - Evidence of DPA review and execution must be retained - Rationale: Article 28 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- PENALTIES FOR AUDIT EVIDENCE FAILURES -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Under Article 83(4), failures to maintain records (Article 30) or cooperate with the supervisory authority (Article 31) can result in fines of up to: - EUR 10,000,000, or - 2% of the total worldwide annual turnover of the preceding financial year, whichever is higher. Under Article 83(5), more serious violations including failure to implement appropriate security measures (Article 32) or failure to carry out a DPIA (Article 35) can result in fines of up to: - EUR 20,000,000, or - 4% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- KEY ARTICLES REFERENCED IN THIS DOCUMENT -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 5(2) — Accountability principle

================================================================================ GDPR — BACKUP AND RECOVERY ================================================================================ OVERVIEW -------- The GDPR does not use the words "backup" or "disaster recovery" explicitly. However, the obligation to maintain backup and recovery capabilities for personal data is clearly and directly derived from several core provisions of the regulation. The primary basis is Article 32, which requires organizations to ensure the ongoing availability and resilience of personal data processing systems and to implement the ability to restore availability and access to personal data in a timely manner after a physical or technical incident. Failure to maintain adequate backup and recovery for personal data is a failure of the security of processing obligation under Article 32. It may also constitute a violation of the integrity and confidentiality principle in Article 5(1)(f). Relevant articles: - Article 5(1)(f): Integrity and confidentiality principle - Article 17: Right to erasure — implications for backed-up data - Article 25: Data protection by design and by default - Article 32: Security of processing — availability and resilience - Article 33: Breach notification — loss of data as a notifiable breach - Article 83: Administrative fines -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 5(1)(f) — INTEGRITY AND CONFIDENTIALITY PRINCIPLE -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text: Personal data shall be "processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures ('integrity and confidentiality')." This is one of the six core data protection principles in the GDPR. The phrase "accidental loss, destruction or damage" directly establishes a legal obligation to protect personal data from being irretrievably lost. This means: 1. Personal data must not be stored in a way that creates a high risk of permanent loss in the event of a system failure, hardware fault, or natural disaster. 2. Backup mechanisms are a direct technical implementation of this principle. 3. The "appropriate" standard is risk-based: more sensitive data (e.g., health records, financial data) requires more robust backup controls than lower-risk data. Cloud security implication: For any cloud service storing personal data, enabling automated backups, versioning, and point-in-time recovery is a direct implementation of Article 5(1)(f). Turning off backup features for cost savings on services handling personal data may constitute a GDPR violation. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 32 — SECURITY OF PROCESSING -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (paragraph 1): "Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature,

Turning off backup features for cost savings on services handling personal data may constitute a GDPR violation. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 32 — SECURITY OF PROCESSING -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (paragraph 1): "Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: (a) the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; (c) the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; (d) a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing." Article 32(1)(b) — Availability and Resilience: This sub-clause establishes a specific obligation for availability. "Availability" means personal data must be accessible when it is needed. "Resilience" means the system must be able to withstand and recover from disruptions. Backup systems are the primary technical mechanism for satisfying this requirement. Article 32(1)(c) — Ability to Restore Availability and Access: This is the most direct GDPR backup obligation. Organizations must have the technical capability to restore access to personal data following a physical or technical incident. Examples of such incidents include: - Hardware failure - Ransomware attacks - Accidental deletion of data - Cloud provider outages - Natural disasters affecting data center infrastructure - Software corruption or database failure The phrase "in a timely manner" means the recovery capability must be active and tested, not merely theoretical. An organization that has never tested its recovery procedures cannot demonstrate this capability. Article 32(1)(d) — Regular Testing: Backup systems must be tested regularly. A backup that has never been tested cannot be relied upon as a compliance measure. Testing evidence (restore test logs, recovery time records) constitutes audit evidence under Article 30 and the accountability principle in Article 5(2). Full

Full text (paragraph 2): "In assessing the appropriate level of security, account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed." This paragraph confirms that accidental destruction and loss are among the primary risks that security measures — including backups — must address. Full text (paragraph 4): "The controller and processor shall take steps to ensure that any natural person acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data does not process them except on instructions from the controller, unless he or she is required to do so by Union or Member State law." This clause also applies to backup systems: personnel with access to backup data must be operating under defined instructions and must not process that data beyond the purpose of backup and recovery. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 25 — DATA PROTECTION BY DESIGN AND BY DEFAULT -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (summary): The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures designed to implement data-protection principles effectively and integrate the necessary safeguards into the processing. Backup and recovery implications: 1. Recovery systems should be designed with data minimisation in mind — backup images should not retain personal data longer than the live system would. 2. Backup access controls should be as strict as production access controls — backups are not a lower-security copy of the data. 3. Encryption should be applied to backup data by default, not as an optional add-on. 4. Backup destinations (e.g., cross-region S3 buckets, Azure backup vaults, GCP backup storage) should be assessed for compliance with data residency requirements before being configured. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 17 — RIGHT TO ERASURE — IMPLICATIONS FOR BACKUP DATA -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (paragraph 1): "The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal

The EDPB (European Data Protection Board) acknowledges that immediate erasure from backups may not always be possible. However, organizations must have a documented approach for handling erasure requests against backup data. 2. BACKUP RETENTION PERIODS MUST BE DEFINED AND JUSTIFIED Backups that retain personal data indefinitely create ongoing GDPR exposure. Backup retention periods must be set and enforced. When a backup lifecycle ends, personal data within it must be securely deleted or made effectively unidentifiable. 3. A Data Processing Agreement must be in place before that provider receives any personal data. The DPA must specify: - What data the backup provider may access - Where it may be stored (geographic restrictions) - Retention periods - Security requirements - The provider's obligation to assist with erasure requests 4. A personal data breach includes the accidental loss or destruction of personal data (Article 4(12) definition: "a breach of security leading to the accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to, personal data"). This means: 1. Loss of personal data because there was no backup, or a backup failed, can constitute a notifiable personal data breach if the loss is likely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals. 2. A ransomware attack that encrypts personal data and destroys it is a breach requiring notification if the data cannot be restored from backup. 3. Accidental deletion of a database containing personal data is a breach if there is no backup enabling recovery. Having functional, tested backups is therefore also a breach-prevention and breach-mitigation measure. The presence of a working backup may reduce the severity of an incident below the notification threshold. Breach documentation (Article 33(5)): Even where notification is not required, all incidents involving potential data loss must be documented in the breach register. text: ================================================================================ GDPR — DATA RETENTION ================================================================================ OVERVIEW -------- Data retention under the GDPR is governed primarily by the storage limitation principle in Article 5(1)(e) and the right to erasure in Article 17. Together, these provisions require organizations to: 1. Keep personal data only for as long as it is necessary for the purpose for which it was collected. 2. Define and document retention periods before or at the time of collection. 3. Securely delete or effectively anonymise personal data when the retention period expires. 4. Apply the same retention obligations to all copies of data — including backups, archives, and cloud snapshots. The GDPR does not specify fixed retention durations for most categories of personal data. Instead, it requires organizations to justify each retention period based on the purpose of processing, applicable legal or regulatory obligations, and the principle of data minimisation. Relevant articles: - Article 5(1)(b): Purpose limitation - Article 5(1)(c): Data minimisation - Article 5(1)(e): Storage limitation - Article 5(2): Accountability — demonstrate retention decisions - Article 6: Lawfulness of processing — basis for retention - Article 13–14: Transparency — inform subjects of retention periods - Article 17: Right to erasure - Article 30: Records of processing — document retention periods - Article 89: Derogations for archiving, research, statistics -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 5(1)(e) — STORAGE LIMITATION PRINCIPLE -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text: Personal data shall be "kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89(1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject ('storage limitation')." This is the core GDPR data retention obligation. Key principles derived from it: 1. TIME-BOUND BY PURPOSE Data must only be held for as long as it serves the purpose for which it was collected. Once the purpose is fulfilled, the legal basis for retention ends and the data must be deleted or anonymised. 2. NO INDEFINITE RETENTION Keeping personal data indefinitely without a specific, documented justification is non-compliant. The absence of a retention schedule is itself a GDPR violation under the accountability principle

GDPR data retention obligation. Key principles derived from it: 1. TIME-BOUND BY PURPOSE Data must only be held for as long as it serves the purpose for which it was collected. Once the purpose is fulfilled, the legal basis for retention ends and the data must be deleted or anonymised. 2. NO INDEFINITE RETENTION Keeping personal data indefinitely without a specific, documented justification is non-compliant. The absence of a retention schedule is itself a GDPR violation under the accountability principle (Article 5(2)). 3. IDENTIFICATION AS THE THRESHOLD The storage limitation applies to data "which permits identification of data subjects." This means: - Truly anonymised data (where re-identification is impossible) falls outside GDPR scope. - Pseudonymised data (e.g., records linked to a tokenized ID) still permits identification and remains within GDPR scope. - Archived or backed-up data containing identifiable personal data remains subject to the storage limitation. 4. ARCHIVING EXCEPTION The storage limitation does not apply to data retained for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research, or statistical purposes — provided: - Appropriate safeguards are implemented (Article 89(1)) - The data is not used for other purposes - Technical measures minimize the risk to data subjects (e.g., pseudonymisation) -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 5(1)(b) — PURPOSE LIMITATION AND RETENTION -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text: Personal data shall be "collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes." Purpose limitation intersects directly with retention: if data is being retained beyond the period needed for its original purpose, continuing to hold it requires either a compatible new purpose or a new lawful basis. Retention purely because the data "might be useful one day" is not a valid purpose. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 5(1)(c) — DATA MINIMISATION AND RETENTION -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text: Personal data shall be "adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed." Data minimisation applies not only to what data is collected but to how long it is held. Holding more data fields, or holding data for longer, than is necessary for the processing purpose violates data minimisation. Organizations must regularly review retained data and delete fields or records that are no longer necessary. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 6 — LAWFUL BASIS AND ITS EFFECT ON RETENTION -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (summary): Processing is lawful only if at least one of the following applies: (a) The data

applies not only to what data is collected but to how long it is held. Holding more data fields, or holding data for longer, than is necessary for the processing purpose violates data minimisation. Organizations must regularly review retained data and delete fields or records that are no longer necessary. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 6 — LAWFUL BASIS AND ITS EFFECT ON RETENTION -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (summary): Processing is lawful only if at least one of the following applies: (a) The data subject has given consent (b) Processing is necessary for the performance of a contract (c) Processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation (d) Processing is necessary to protect vital interests (e) Processing is necessary for a task in the public interest (f) Processing is necessary for the legitimate interests of the controller The lawful basis chosen for processing determines the retention period: CONSENT (6(1)(a)): Retention is valid only as long as consent remains in force. When consent is withdrawn, the lawful basis ends and the data must be deleted unless another basis applies. Consent records themselves should be retained to demonstrate that consent was valid during the period of processing. CONTRACT (6(1)(b)): Data collected for contract performance can be retained for the duration of the contract and for a reasonable period thereafter to handle post-contract queries, disputes, or legal claims. Common retention period: contract duration plus applicable statute of limitations (varies by jurisdiction). LEGAL OBLIGATION (6(1)(c)): Specific legal or regulatory requirements may mandate minimum retention periods (e.g., tax records 6–7 years, employment records 6 years in the UK). These obligations override the storage limitation for the required period but do not justify retention beyond it. LEGITIMATE INTERESTS (6(1)(f)): Retention must be no longer than is necessary for the legitimate interest. Balancing test results and retention justification should be documented. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 13 AND 14 — TRANSPARENCY: INFORMING DATA SUBJECTS OF RETENTION PERIODS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (Article 13(2)(a)): At the time personal data are collected, the controller shall provide the data subject with "the period for which the personal data will be stored, or if that is not possible, the criteria used to determine that period." Full text (Article 14(3)(a)): The same obligation applies where data was not obtained directly from the data subject. Implications: 1. Retention periods must be determined before data is collected — not after. 2. Where a specific period cannot be stated, the criteria for

Full text (Article 14(3)(a)): The same obligation applies where data was not obtained directly from the data subject. Implications: 1. Retention periods must be determined before data is collected — not after. 2. Where a specific period cannot be stated, the criteria for determining the period must be explained (e.g., "retained for the duration of the contract plus 6 years"). 3. Privacy notices and consent forms must reflect the actual retention period. 4. If the retention period changes, data subjects must be informed. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 17 — RIGHT TO ERASURE ('RIGHT TO BE FORGOTTEN') -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (paragraph 1): "The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: (a) the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; (b) the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based and there is no other legal ground for the processing; (c) the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21(1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects pursuant to Article 21(2); (d) the personal data have been unlawfully processed; (e) the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law; (f) the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8(1)." The right to erasure is not absolute. It may be overridden where processing is necessary for: - Exercising freedom of expression and information (Article 17(3)(a)) - Compliance with a legal obligation (Article 17(3)(b)) - Public health purposes (Article 17(3)(c)) - Archiving in the public interest, scientific/historical research, or statistics (Article 17(3)(d)) - Establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims (Article 17(3)(e)) Erasure vs. anonymisation: - Where complete deletion is not feasible (e.g., data embedded in backup archives), effective anonymisation — removing all direct and indirect identifiers so re-identification is not possible — is an accepted alternative.

This means: - The retention schedule for each data category must be documented in the Record of Processing Activities (RoPA). - This documentation must be available to the supervisory authority on request. - If erasure time limits cannot be specified precisely, the criteria for determining them must be stated. A complete retention schedule in the RoPA is strong evidence that the organization is managing personal data in a structured and accountable way. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 89 — DEROGATIONS FOR ARCHIVING, RESEARCH, AND STATISTICS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Full text (summary): Processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes, or statistical purposes shall be subject to appropriate safeguards (e.g., pseudonymisation). Member States may provide further derogations from certain data subject rights in these contexts. Conditions for invoking the Article 89 derogation: 1. The primary purpose of retention must genuinely be archiving, research, or statistics — not general business purposes. 2. Appropriate safeguards must be implemented (typically pseudonymisation and access controls). 3. The derogation cannot be used as a general escape from the storage limitation principle. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ESTABLISHING A GDPR-COMPLIANT RETENTION SCHEDULE -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- A compliant retention schedule must: 1. IDENTIFY ALL DATA CATEGORIES Map every category of personal data processed by the organization, including data held in cloud databases, object storage, backups, logs, and archives. 2. DETERMINE THE LAWFUL BASIS FOR EACH CATEGORY Document which Article 6 basis applies to each data category and how it affects the retention period. 3. IDENTIFY APPLICABLE LEGAL RETENTION REQUIREMENTS Some personal data is subject to mandatory minimum retention periods under sector-specific law (e.g., financial, employment, health). These override the storage

APPLY RETENTION TO ALL COPIES Retention periods apply equally to production data, backups, archives, logs, and cloud snapshots. Each must be included in the retention schedule. 7. REVIEW AND UPDATE REGULARLY Retention schedules should be reviewed at least annually and when processing purposes change. 8. DOCUMENT AND MAKE AVAILABLE The schedule must be recorded in the RoPA (Article 30) and reflected in privacy notices (Articles 13–14). -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- CLOUD CONFIGURATION IMPLICATIONS — SUMMARY -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- GDPR retention obligations translate directly into the following cloud configuration requirements: 1. OBJECT STORAGE LIFECYCLE POLICIES - AWS S3: Configure Lifecycle Rules to automatically expire objects after the defined retention period. Set expiration actions to permanently delete objects. Use Intelligent-Tiering for cost efficiency during the retention period where access frequency is variable. - Azure Blob Storage: Configure Blob Lifecycle Management policies with delete actions after defined number of days since last modification or creation. Enable immutability policies with defined retention intervals for compliance-locked data. - GCP Cloud Storage: Configure Object Lifecycle Management rules with Age condition and Delete action to enforce retention periods. Use retention policies on buckets to prevent deletion before minimum retention period expires. 2. DATABASE RETENTION - Implement application-level data purging routines aligned with the retention schedule. - For RDS/Cloud SQL/Azure SQL: implement scheduled jobs to delete expired personal data records. - Ensure purging logic covers all tables and fields holding personal data, not just primary user records. - Test purging routines and retain records of execution as audit evidence. 3. LOG RETENTION - Logs containing personal data (e.g., application logs with user identifiers, IP addresses, or behavioral data) are subject to GDPR retention limits. - Define log retention periods and configure CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, or GCP Cloud Logging accordingly. - Minimum retention for security audit logs: 12 months operational plus longer archival if required for breach evidence. - Logs should be reviewed for personal data content. Excessive personal data in logs (e.g., full request payloads containing user data) should be reduced at collection time through sanitization or redaction. 4. This includes automated deletion of snapshot chains and backup vault entries. 5. ENCRYPTION AND KEY MANAGEMENT FOR RETENTION ENFORCEMENT - Cryptographic erasure: deleting the encryption key renders encrypted personal data irrecoverable and constitutes effective erasure where direct deletion is not feasible. - AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, and GCP Cloud KMS support key deletion with configurable grace periods (minimum 7–30 days depending on provider). - Cryptographic erasure must be documented to serve as audit evidence that the data is no longer accessible. 6. AUDIT AND REPORTING - Maintain records of data deletions and lifecycle policy executions. - Generate periodic reports showing that retention schedules are being enforced in practice. - These reports are audit evidence under Article 5(2) accountability. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- SPECIAL CATEGORIES OF DATA — HEIGHTENED RETENTION OBLIGATIONS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 9 defines special categories of personal data requiring heightened protection, including: - Health data - Genetic and biometric data - Racial or ethnic origin - Political opinions - Religious or philosophical beliefs - Trade union membership - Sexual orientation and sex life data For special categories: - Retention periods should generally be shorter unless a specific legal obligation requires longer retention. - Deletion processes must be more rigorous

Under Article 83(4), violations of recordkeeping obligations (Article 30) including failure to document retention periods can result in fines of up to: - EUR 10,000,000, or - 2% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- KEY ARTICLES REFERENCED IN THIS DOCUMENT -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 4(1) — Definition of personal data Article 4(5) — Definition of pseudonymisation Article 5(1)(b) — Purpose limitation Article 5(1)(c) — Data minimisation Article 5(1)(e) — Storage limitation Article 5(2) — Accountability Article 6 — Lawfulness of processing Article 9 — Special categories of personal data Article 13 — Transparency (collected directly from subject) Article 14 — Transparency (not collected from subject) Article 17 — Right to erasure Article 30 — Records of processing activities Article 89 — Archiving and research derogations Article 83 — Administrative fines

================================================================================ GDPR ENCRYPTION REQUIREMENTS ================================================================================ OVERVIEW -------- The GDPR does not mandate a specific encryption algorithm or strength but identifies encryption as an explicitly recommended technical measure in multiple articles. It also treats the presence of encryption as a mitigating factor in personal data breach notification obligations. The regulation's risk-based approach means that encryption is practically required for any personal data exposed to meaningful risk. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 4 - DEFINITIONS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 4(5) - Pseudonymisation: 'pseudonymisation' means the processing of personal data in such a manner that the personal data can no longer be attributed to a specific data subject without the use of additional information, provided that such additional information is kept separately and is subject to technical and organisational measures to ensure that the personal data are not attributed to an identified or identifiable natural person. Note: Pseudonymisation is distinct from encryption but is frequently implemented via encryption. The GDPR treats pseudonymised data as still being personal data unless the re-identification key is permanently destroyed. Recital 26: Personal data which have undergone pseudonymisation, which could be attributed to a natural person by the use of additional information, should be considered to be information on an identifiable natural person. Recital 28: The application of pseudonymisation to personal data can reduce the risks to the data subjects concerned and help controllers and processors to meet their data-protection obligations. The explicit introduction of 'pseudonymisation' in this Regulation is not intended to preclude any other measures of data protection. Recital 29: In order to create incentives to apply pseudonymisation when processing personal data, measures of pseudonymisation should, whilst allowing general analysis, be possible within the same controller when that controller has taken technical and organisational measures necessary to ensure, for the processing concerned, that this Regulation is implemented, and that additional information for attributing the personal data to a specific data subject is kept separately. ================================================================================ GDPR IDENTITY AND AUTHENTICATION REQUIREMENTS ================================================================================ OVERVIEW -------- The GDPR does not define specific authentication protocols but establishes obligations that can only be satisfied through strong identity and authentication controls. The key obligations are: ensuring only authorised persons can access personal data (Article 32), applying data protection by default (Article 25), verifying the identity of data subjects making access requests (Article 12), and demonstrating accountability (Article 5(2)). Recital 64 explicitly addresses identity verification in the context of online services. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 4 - DEFINITIONS (RELEVANT TO IDENTITY) -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 4(1) - Personal Data: 'personal data' means any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person ('data subject'); an identifiable natural person is one who can be identified, directly or indirectly, in particular by reference to an identifier such as a name, an online identifier or to one or more factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural or social identity of that natural person. Article 4(5) - Pseudonymisation: 'pseudonymisation' means the processing of personal data in such a manner that the personal data can no longer be attributed to a specific data subject without the use of additional information, provided that such additional information is kept separately and is subject to technical and organisational measures to ensure that the personal data are not attributed to an identified or identifiable natural person. Note: Identifiers used for authentication (user IDs, session tokens, API keys) may themselves constitute personal data if they can identify a natural person. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 5 - INTEGRITY AND CONFIDENTIALITY PRINCIPLE -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 5(1)(f): Personal data shall be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures ('integrity and confidentiality'). Practical Implication: Authentication is the gateway to the "authorised" access requirement. Without strong authentication, the confidentiality and integrity principle cannot be met. Weak authentication (e.g., no MFA, default credentials, shared passwords) directly violates Article 5(1)(f). -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 25 - DATA PROTECTION BY DESIGN AND BY DEFAULT -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 25(1): The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures, such as pseudonymisation, which are designed to implement data-protection principles in an effective manner and to integrate the necessary safeguards into the processing. Article 25(2): The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures for ensuring that, by default, only personal data which are

Weak authentication (e.g., no MFA, default credentials, shared passwords) directly violates Article 5(1)(f). -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 25 - DATA PROTECTION BY DESIGN AND BY DEFAULT -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 25(1): The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures, such as pseudonymisation, which are designed to implement data-protection principles in an effective manner and to integrate the necessary safeguards into the processing. Article 25(2): The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures for ensuring that, by default, only personal data which are necessary for each specific purpose of the processing are processed. In particular, such measures shall ensure that by default personal data are not made accessible without the individual's intervention to an indefinite number of natural persons. Cloud Implication: - IAM roles must follow least privilege as the default; overly broad roles must require explicit justification and approval. - Service accounts must not be assigned human-level permissions. - Root/owner accounts must not be used for routine operations; dedicated IAM users/roles must be created for all workloads. - MFA must be enforced by default for all human access to cloud management consoles and APIs that can reach personal data. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 29 - PROCESSING UNDER THE AUTHORITY OF THE CONTROLLER -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 29: The processor and any person acting under the authority of the controller or of the processor who has access to personal data shall not process those data except on instructions from the controller, unless required to do so by Union or Member State law. Practical Implication: Only persons who are identified, authenticated, and acting under documented instructions may process personal data. Anonymous or shared-credential access to personal data is incompatible with this requirement because it is impossible to attribute processing actions to a specific natural person acting under instructions. Cloud Implication: - No shared IAM users or shared credentials for accessing personal data. - Each person must have an individual identity with their own credentials. - Service-to-service authentication must use roles or managed identities, not shared static credentials. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 32 - SECURITY OF PROCESSING -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 32(1): The controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including as appropriate: (a) the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services. Article 32(4): The controller and processor shall take steps to ensure

use roles or managed identities, not shared static credentials. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 32 - SECURITY OF PROCESSING -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 32(1): The controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including as appropriate: (a) the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services. Article 32(4): The controller and processor shall take steps to ensure that any natural person acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data does not process them except on instructions from the controller, unless required to do so by Union or Member State law. Recital 83: The controller or processor should evaluate the risks inherent in the processing and implement measures to mitigate those risks, such as encryption. Those measures should ensure an appropriate level of security, including confidentiality, taking into account the state of the art. Cloud Implication: - "State of the art" authentication for cloud environments means MFA using hardware tokens, authenticator apps (TOTP), or FIDO2/WebAuthn. - Password-only authentication is not considered "state of the art" for systems processing personal data under current EU supervisory authority guidance. - IAM password policies must enforce minimum length, complexity, and rotation. - Privileged access (admin roles) must require stronger authentication than standard roles (step-up MFA or just-in-time access). -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ARTICLE 12 - VERIFICATION OF DATA SUBJECT IDENTITY FOR ACCESS REQUESTS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Article 12(1): The controller shall take appropriate measures to provide any information referred to in Articles 13 and 14 and any communication under Articles 15 to 22 and 34 relating to processing to the data subject in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language. Article 12(6): Without prejudice to Article 11, where the controller has reasonable doubts concerning the identity of the natural person making the request referred to in Articles 15 to 21, the controller may request the provision of additional information necessary to confirm the identity of the data subject. Recital 64: The controller should use all reasonable measures to verify the identity of a data subject who requests access, in particular in the context of online services and online identifiers.
